channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: How Jonathan Groff of ‘Just in Time’ Became Broadway’s Leading Man
item.description: By transmitting his love of live performance, the “Just in Time” actor has completed his ascendance to full musical stardom.
item.dc:creator: Ben Brantley and Mohamed Sadek
item.media:credit: Mohamed Sadek for The New York Times

item.title: At the Whitney, a Biennial Gets Personal
item.description: For an unmoored time, 56 artists and teams present an inspired discourse shaped by crisis, craft and community. Look up, and listen.
item.dc:creator: Holland Cotter
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times

item.title: Britney Spears Is Arrested on Suspicion of D.U.I. in California
item.description: “Hopefully this can be the first step in long overdue change that needs to occur in Britney’s life,” a representative for the pop star said after she was released from jail.
item.dc:creator: Michaela Towfighi and Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Valerie Macon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Britney Spears was pulled over after officers observed her driving erratically at high speeds, a California Highway Patrol spokesman said.

item.title: Harry Styles Left as a Dominant Male Pop Star. He Returns to a Crowd.
item.description: In the four years since the British singer last released an album, artists like Sombr, working in similar aesthetic modes, have climbed onto the charts.
item.dc:creator: Shaad D’Souza
item.category: Kiss All the Time. Disco Occasionally. (Album)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Druski Talks About His Influences, Hollywood Ambitions and Timothée Chalamet
item.description: The comedian is building a galaxy of collaborators — Timothée Chalamet, Kai Cenat and Justin Bieber — and telling us how they fit into his future.
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica and Joe Coscarelli
item.media:credit: Jason Nocito for The New York Times

item.title: Pokémon Company Objects to White House’s Political Memes
item.description: “No permission was granted for the use of our intellectual property,” the company said. The Trump administration frequently promotes policies with content from video games.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The White House used an image from the new game Pokémon Pokopia in a social media post saying “Make America Great Again.”

item.title: Olivier Awards: ‘Paddington’ and ‘Into the Woods’ Lead Nominations
item.description: The two musicals secured 11 nominations each for Britain’s equivalent of the Tony Awards. Cate Blanchett, Bryan Cranston and Rachel Zegler are also among the nominees.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Kalpesh Lathigra for The New York Times
item.media:description: The 11 nominations for “Paddington,” which is at the Savoy Theater, included one for best new musical.

item.title: The Creators of Halo and Destiny Go Cutthroat With Marathon
item.description: Bungie was bought for billions after creating the hit video games Halo and Destiny. But will players welcome its confrontational extraction shooter Marathon with open arms?
item.dc:creator: Sam Machkovech

item.title: Chadwick Boseman’s Hip-Hop Play, in Shakespeare’s House
item.description: The actor Chadwick Boseman was a playwright, too. At Shakespeare’s Globe in London, his “Deep Azure” is drawing attention to a lost talent inspired by the Bard.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Magdalena Wosinska for The New York Times
item.media:description: Boseman studied theater at Howard University before becoming an actor.

item.title: Squeeze Wrote a Rock Opera Five Decades Ago. It’s Coming Out Now.
item.description: During its early days, the band came up with songs about a fictional nightclub. After a career filled with ups and downs, it finally returned to “Trixies.”
item.dc:creator: David Peisner
item.media:credit: Jono White for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chris Difford and Glenn Tilbrook of Squeeze. Their new (old) album, “Trixies,” is a fascinating window into the group’s musical roots.

item.title: ‘The Bride!’ Review: Jessie Buckley and Christian Bale Are Outlaws in Love
item.description: Maggie Gyllenhaal’s time-shifting, genre-hopping riff on Mary Shelley’s creation stars Jessie Buckley and Christian Bale as outlaws in love.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: ‘Hoppers’ Review: Animal Magnetism
item.description: The latest Pixar movie centers on pudgy beavers and a spunky teenager determined to save their habitat.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: ‘Pompei: Below the Clouds’ Review: Trembling Beauties, Old and New
item.description: The past and the present converge in this ravishingly beautiful Italian documentary set in the shadow of Mount Vesuvius.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: ‘Theory of Flames’ Dives Down the Rabbit Hole of Paranoia
item.description: Michel van der Aa’s “Theory of Flames” originates from a question: “How can we relate to people if we don’t believe in the same reality?”
item.dc:creator: Hugh Morris and Desiré Van Den Berg
item.media:credit: Desiré van den Berg for The New York Times
item.media:description: Michel van der Aa’s film opera “Theory of Flames,” premiering at the Dutch National Opera on Friday, centers on a troubled protagonist’s descent into conspiracy theories.

item.title: Late Night Isn’t Sad to See Kristi Noem Leave Homeland Security
item.description: “Sounds like someone’s about to become the FIFA secretary of homeland security,” Stephen Colbert joked.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: ‘The Pitt’ Season 2, Episode 9 Recap: Family Separation
item.description: A young boy arrives at the emergency room this week with a firework-related injury, but his troubles run much deeper than that.
item.dc:creator: Sean T. Collins
item.media:credit: Warrick Page/HBO Max
item.media:description: From left, Sasha Compere, Noah Wyle and Isa Briones in “The Pitt.” Episode 9 included a story line in which a patient’s parents were seized during an immigration hearing and deported to Haiti.

item.title: Wallace Shawn’s ‘What We Did Before Our Moth Days’ Is Purgatory Done Right
item.description: The playwright and his collaborator André Gregory are together again, delivering a sumptuous set of interlinked monologues about life, death and betrayal.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.category: What We Did Before Our Moth Days (Play)
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: From left, Hope Davis, John Early, Josh Hamilton and Maria Dizzia in “What We Did Before Our Moth Days” at the Greenwich House Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Art Gallery Shows to See in March
item.description: This week in Newly Reviewed, Seph Rodney covers Deborah Roberts’s collages, Ursula von Rydingsvard’s wood outcroppings and Noel W Anderson’s superstars.
item.dc:creator: Seph Rodney

item.title: António Lobo Antunes, One of Europe’s Most Revered Writers, Dies at 83
item.description: In a career studded with literary awards, he was the author of dozens of books that grappled with his nation’s legacy of dictatorship and colonialism.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:credit: Ulf Andersen/Getty Images
item.media:description: António Lobo Antunes in 1988. The literary critic George Steiner called him “a novelist of the very first rank” and likened him to Joseph Conrad and William Faulkner.

item.title: Megumi Yuasa’s Six-Decade-Long Love Affair With Clay
item.description: At 87, the sculptor and ceramist Megumi Yuasa is having his first solo exhibition in the U.S.
item.dc:creator: Rose Courteau and Luisa Dörr

item.title: Paris Again Merges as a Mecca for the Arts
item.description: Many of the galleries showing at TEFAF Maastricht are from France, a sign of the country’s growing presence in fairs and overall rising influence in art and collecting.
item.dc:creator: Tariro Mzezewa
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We are still very open in Paris,” said Nicolas Fournery, a gallerist who is showing at TEFAF for the second year. “When you compare with the United States and other countries, they are closing, but we are not in the same mood in France.”

item.title: This Small Statue in Brussels Has a Large Bladder and Lots of Outfits
item.description: Manneken Pis, the famed bronze statue of a little boy who always has to go, has an official dresser in charge of his more than 1,190 outfits.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal
item.category: TEFAF (The European Fine Art Fair Stichting)

item.title: How the Florists Behind TEFAF Maastricht Work Their Magic
item.description: A Dutch florist has crafted the fair’s massive, colorful arrangements since 1988. Their secrets? Early mornings, intense planning and thousands of flowers.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Morelli
item.category: TEFAF (The European Fine Art Fair Stichting)

item.title: Welcome to Maastricht, Where All Things French Flourish
item.description: Evident is the vast influence that France has had upon the Netherlands city, the result of its geography, history and the population’s affinity for its culture.
item.dc:creator: Elaine Sciolino and Ilvy Njiokiktjien
item.media:description: The City Hall tower in Maastricht, the southernmost city in the Netherlands.

item.title: Margrethe Odgaard’s Explorations of Color Come to TEFAF
item.description: Margrethe Odgaard, whose new textiles will be featured at TEFAF Maastricht, aims to define the distinctive hues preferred in various places, by different cultures.
item.dc:creator: Alec Scott
item.category: TEFAF (The European Fine Art Fair Stichting)
item.media:credit: Carsten Snejbjerg for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Danish designer and textile artist Margrethe Odgaard, who has helped create colors for many brands and products, is now using hand-dyed fabrics to create art pieces.

item.title: TEFAF Maastricht, Known for Old Art, Faces the Future
item.description: The Dutch art fair, renowned for art and antiquities, works to prove its relevance as the wider world changes around it.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn
item.category: TEFAF (The European Fine Art Fair Stichting)

item.title: At TEFAF Maastricht, a Paris Gallery Honors a Revolutionary French Painter
item.description: In the ’70s, Patrick Saytour and his fellow avant-gardists took their work outside the frame, exploding the notion of what painting might be.
item.dc:creator: Dale Berning Sawa
item.category: TEFAF (The European Fine Art Fair Stichting)

item.title: More Than TEFAF Maastricht, Other Cities Are on Display
item.description: Nearby cities in the Netherlands and Belgium will display van Gogh masterpieces, an exhibition on birds, an exploration of the evolution of beauty standards and more.
item.dc:creator: Farah Nayeri

item.title: At TEFAF, Photography Displays Add a Contemporary Flavor
item.description: As the Maastricht fair continues to update its outlook, an emphasis on photography is bringing together “the whole range of what culture can be.”
item.dc:creator: Jillian Rayfield
item.category: TEFAF (The European Fine Art Fair Stichting)

item.title: A Mexico City Design Gallery Hits the World Stage
item.description: AGO Projects, which specializes in contemporary work, recently turned heads by standing by an artist who had become a political lightning rod.
item.dc:creator: Ray Mark Rinaldi
item.category: TEFAF (The European Fine Art Fair Stichting)
item.media:credit: Greta Rico for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rudy Weissenberg, left, and Rodman Primack in their Mexico City design gallery, AGO Projects. Weissenberg and Primack, longtime personal and professional partners, opened AGO six years ago.

item.title: ‘Protector’ Review: She Knows How to Give Life, and How to Take It
item.description: Milla Jovovich plays a mother whose wrath is unleashed when her daughter is kidnapped in this brooding revenge thriller.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: Russia Returns to Venice Biennale, in Latest Sign of a Cultural Comeback
item.description: By hosting a pavilion again this year, Russia continues its efforts to shed its status as a cultural and sporting pariah.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: In 2024, Russia lent its Venice Biennale pavilion to Bolivia rather than taking part in the event.

item.title: The Statues Were Mostly Men or Nude Women. So These Knitters Got to Work.
item.description: Women in Denmark, dismayed by unequal representation in public art, stitched together a protest campaign.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Abend

item.title: ‘Youngblood’ Review: He’s Got a Reason to Be Chippy
item.description: A remake of the 1986 crowd-pleasing hockey movie, starring Ashton James as a promising young Black player, has a lot more on its mind than the original.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: ‘The Napa Boys’ Review: You Had Me at Merlot
item.description: This unclassifiable postmodern spoof, from the comedians Nick Corirossi and Armen Weitzman, is a strange, circuitous trip through California wine country.
item.dc:creator: Calum Marsh

item.title: ‘Heel’ Review: A Different Kind of House Training
item.description: A loutish teenager is shown the error of his ways in this absurdly gripping psychological thriller.
item.dc:creator: Jeannette Catsoulis

item.title: ‘André Is an Idiot’ Review: Dying Laughing
item.description: In this documentary, an ad man who treats life with irreverence tries to approach his death from colon cancer the same way.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: ‘Dolly’ Review: Toy With Me
item.description: A backwoods monster with a twisted doll obsession puts a couple through the wringer in this unflinching exploitation fairy tale.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg
item.media:credit: John Blazzi/IFC Films and Shudder
item.media:description: Max the Impaler, left, and Fabianne Therese in “Dolly.”

item.title: Late Night Doesn’t Understand Why America Is Attacking Iran
item.description: “This could be the first war ever launched based on vibes,” Jimmy Fallon said.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Sarah J. Mass Announces Two New ‘ACOTAR’ Books on ‘Call Her Daddy’ Podcast
item.description: The sixth book is scheduled to be released on Oct. 27, 2026, and the seventh on Jan. 12, 2027, the author announced on the “Call Her Daddy” podcast.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler
item.category: A Court of Thorns and Roses (Book)
item.media:credit: Cindy Ord/Getty Images
item.media:description: Sarah J. Maas did not share the titles or cover art for her new books, but acknowledged that the new installments had been “a long time coming.”

item.title: Former Barclays Center Executive Says Live Nation Threatened to Pull Tours
item.description: The Justice Department had John Abbamondi testify at an antitrust trial to support its case that Live Nation has acted as a monopoly, an accusation the company denies.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Barclays Center in Brooklyn was the subject of heated testimony at the Live Nation trial on Wednesday.

item.title: The Best Movies and TV Shows Coming to Disney+, Hulu, Apple TV and More in March
item.description: A new Bill Lawrence comedy starring Steve Carell and a new mystery starring Nicole Kidman are among this month’s highlights.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.media:credit: Connie Chornuk/Prime
item.media:description: Nicole Kidman plays a Virginia medical examiner in “Scarpetta,” based on the character created by Patricia Cornwell.

item.title: Bob Power, 73, Hip-Hop Engineer and Tribe Called Quest Collaborator, Dies
item.description: He helped pioneer a newly complex approach to sampling in rap music, including on A Tribe Called Quest’s influential 1991 album “The Low End Theory.”
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Cindy Ord/WireImage for The Recording Academy, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Bob Power in 2016. “Great music is made by people who either don’t care or don’t understand what is ‘normal,’ so they do something extraordinary,” he said.

item.title: Christian Astuguevieille, 79, Dies; Created Strange Scents and Enigmatic Objects
item.description: For Commes des Garçons, he designed improbable perfumes that conjured burning rubber and cars leaking oil. His uncanny art pieces were equally contrarian.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green
item.media:credit: Marie Pierre Morel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Christian Astuguevieille at home in Paris in 1995, with his chests of knotted and tufted cord. “Altering and deforming the original use of things is important to me,” he told The New York Times in 2011. “I love to mystify.”

item.title: John P. Hammond, Pioneer in 1960s Blues Renaissance, Dies at 83
item.description: With his acclaimed interpretations of Delta Blues standards, he was a fixture on the Greenwich Village music scene for decades.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: The Estate of Edmund Teske, via Getty Images
item.media:description: John P. Hammond in 1962. “When I first heard blues, I was completely turned on to it and it became larger than life,” he said. “And then it became my life.”

item.title: To Many, the Buildings Are Incredible Hulks. They’re Doomed Anyway.
item.description: Why the country is quick to tear down its modern architectural masterpieces.
item.dc:creator: Naomi Pollock

item.title: Fusing the Personal and the Political, With Monumental Results
item.description: Doron Langberg used to think their Israeli heritage was incidental to their art. Then the Gaza war brought questions of identity and history to the surface.
item.dc:creator: Julia Halperin and Justin J Wee
item.media:description: Doron Langberg in their Queens studio, sitting in front of their painting “Bronica Forest.”

item.title: Hugh Jackman in ‘Sexual Misconduct’ and 23 Other Off Broadway Shows to See in March
item.description: Hugh Jackman returns in “Sexual Misconduct of the Middle Classes,” Jesse Tyler Ferguson plays Truman Capote, and Celia Keenan-Bolger and Tony Shalhoub star in an “Antigone” riff.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.category: Sexual Misconduct of the Middle Classes (Play)
Antigone (This Play I Read in High School) (Play)

item.title: Watch a Haunting Theatrical Scene From ‘Hamnet’
item.description: The director Chloé Zhao narrates a sequence from her film featuring Jessie Buckley and Paul Mescal. The movie is nominated for eight Oscars, including best picture.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy
item.media:credit: Agata Grzybowska/Focus Features
item.media:description: Jessie Buckley, center, in a scene from “Hamnet.”

item.title: Did You Know These Oscar-Nominated Actresses Started in Reality TV?
item.description: Hollywood careers don’t always begin where you expect them to, as Jessie Buckley, Teyana Taylor and Emma Stone could tell you.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan

item.title: 5 Minutes That Will Make You Love Ray Barretto
item.description: A son of Puerto Rican parents who grew up in the Bronx, Barretto moved between jazz and Latin music with relentless creativity. Hear 12 indestructible tracks.
item.dc:creator: Giovanni Russonello

item.title: On ‘You’re Free to Go,’ Anjimile Explores Rebirth and Transformation
item.description: The singer-songwriter’s latest album, “You’re Free to Go,” has poppier influences undergirding its signature gut punch.
item.dc:creator: David Peisner
item.media:credit: Sebastian Siadecki for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Living takes work,” Anjimile said. “Sometimes, it’s really hard but I feel like I have a base line now of ‘I’m going to be OK.’”

item.title: ‘Outlander’ Brought the World to Scotland, and Scotland to the World
item.description: The hit show is wrapping up, but its legacy will live on in a tourism boom and a blossoming local TV industry.
item.dc:creator: Imogen West-Knights and Ellie Smith
item.media:credit: Ellie Smith for The New York Times

item.title: The Many Brides of Frankenstein
item.description: Maggie Gyllenhaal’s “The Bride!” imagines an empowered mate for the monster. We look back at other memorable cinematic versions.
item.dc:creator: Robert Ito

item.title: Did Andrea Fraser’s Career Bloom Because Her Mother’s Career Died?
item.description: Andrea Fraser had long felt that she was to blame for the years her mother, Carmen de Monteflores, was overlooked. Now Carmen is 92. Can the Whitney Biennial make amends?
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small and Lucia Bell-Epstein
item.media:description: “If I hold this unconscious conviction that I destroyed my mother’s creativity,” Fraser said, “then this latest effort is about trying to repair things decades later.”

item.title: Why Bethany Collins Transcribed ‘Moby-Dick’ by Hand
item.description: For Bethany Collins, Herman Melville’s novel is rife with centuries-old political anxieties that still resonate today.
item.dc:creator: Elly Fishman
item.category: Museum of Contemporary Art Denver (MCA Denver)

item.title: ‘Hamnet’ | Anatomy of a Scene
item.description: The director Chloé Zhao narrates a sequence from her Oscar-nominated film, featuring Jessie Buckley and Paul Mescal.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy
item.media:credit: Agata Grzybowska/Focus Features
item.media:description: Jessie Buckley, center, in a scene from “Hamnet.”

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Wants to Host the White House Correspondents' Dinner
item.description: “Please, give the people what they want for once,” the talk show host appealed to President Trump. “This is our destiny together, you and me. Think about it.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: ‘Cheap’ and ‘Appalling’: Trump’s Ballroom Plans Receive a Flood of Negative Comments
item.description: The National Capital Planning Commission received about 32,000 messages during its public comment period. Suffice it to say: Many people are not happy with the president’s ballroom plans.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Dylan Freedman
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s demolition of the East Wing and remaking of the White House in his own style have struck a nerve with Americans.

item.title: Live Nation Accused of Hurting Music Fans as Antitrust Trial Begins
item.description: The Justice Department lawsuit says the concert giant acts as a monopoly in the music industry, a charge the company denies.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Mike Blake/Reuters
item.media:description: The Trump Administration is pursuing a major antitrust case against Live Nation, the concert giant who government lawyers say unfairly dominates the music industry.
